header.stance: Restrictive real-rate pressure persists as oil and Fed repricing meet firm duration demand
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The July 8 Treasury close extended the prior day’s inflation-and-supply selloff, but in a narrower and more controlled form. The five-year sector led the cheapening, the two-year remained sensitive to a renewed tightening path, and benchmark and long yields rose only slightly after a well-supported 10-year reopening. The 10-year finished at 4.56%, one basis point higher, as its real yield increased and its breakeven was unchanged. That composition kept the central message anchored in restrictive policy and required real compensation, not an unmoored rise in long-run inflation expectations.

Energy and Federal Reserve communication supplied the dominant macro channel. Brent gained 5.21% as the US-Iran ceasefire faltered, fresh US strikes and restrictions on Iranian oil sales revived supply risk, and thin product inventories increased the potential pass-through from crude into refined fuels. The June FOMC minutes then showed that a few officials had seen a case for an increase even though the committee unanimously held rates steady. Money markets brought the next expected hike forward to October. Together, those developments kept the front end from finding relief even as same-day household-credit data pointed to softer borrowing momentum.

Duration demand provided the main counterforce. The $39 billion 10-year reopening drew a 2.59 bid-to-cover ratio and cleared slightly through pre-auction trading, with indirect accounts taking most accepted competitive bids. That outcome did not reverse the global selloff, yet it prevented the energy shock and hawkish minutes from becoming another indiscriminate rise across the curve. The prior close had exposed how Treasury supply and a jumbo Amazon financing could compete for balance-sheet capacity; Wednesday showed that higher yields could still attract benchmark buyers.

The result was a continuation of the restrictive-rate regime with an important change in intensity. Oil, global policy repricing and large financing needs still demanded a high clearing rate, while stronger auction absorption, flat 10-year inflation compensation and a late recovery in US equities limited the stress signal. Credit remained open but differentiated, with broad risk gauges only modestly weaker and distressed borrowers facing much harsher terms. Japan’s weak currency, rising yields and unresolved fiscal funding added a durable international pressure point. The close therefore described a market repricing policy persistence and real duration risk, not one losing confidence in the inflation anchor or approaching a generalized funding break.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasury cheapening was concentrated in the belly. The two-year yield rose two basis points to 4.21%, the five-year increased four to 4.31%, and both the 10- and 30-year yields added one basis point, ending at 4.56% and 5.06%. The five-year’s underperformance captured the intersection of a nearer Fed hike, resilient activity and a policy path that could remain restrictive beyond one meeting. The long end stayed above 5%, but its smaller daily move showed that Wednesday added less incremental term-premium pressure than Tuesday’s synchronized selloff.

The curve flattened slightly against the front end even though it remained positively sloped. The 2s10s spread narrowed one basis point to 35, and 2s30s also narrowed one to 85. The 2s5s10s butterfly rose five basis points to minus 15 as the five-year cheapened sharply against its wings. By contrast, the 2s10s30s butterfly slipped one basis point to minus 15. Those changes identify a policy-sensitive belly adjustment rather than a new long-end-led fiscal steepening, even as the absolute 30-year yield continued to embody a high structural hurdle.

Inflation-linked Treasuries sharpened that interpretation. The 10-year real yield rose one basis point to 2.31%, while 10-year breakeven inflation held at 2.25%. Five-year real yields increased one basis point to 2.00%, seven-year real yields were unchanged at 2.14%, and the 20- and 30-year real yields rose one and two basis points to 2.66% and 2.86%. Nominal cheapening therefore came through real compensation across the benchmark and long sectors. Oil altered the policy distribution, but it did not generate a same-day rise in the 10-year inflation measure.

The FOMC minutes explained why the energy shock mattered more to the front end than a transitory-oil framework alone would imply. Officials judged upside risks to price stability elevated and downside employment risks slightly reduced at the June 16-17 meeting. A few saw a case for raising rates, although all supported keeping the federal funds range at 3.5% to 3.75%. Nine officials projected at least one quarter-point increase in 2026, including six who anticipated at least two; the other nine expected no move or a cut. Chair Kevin Warsh did not submit a forecast.

The discussion was explicitly conditional. Most participants could envisage holding or eventually lowering rates if inflation moderated, yet most also saw some firming as likely if AI-driven demand, energy and tariffs kept inflation elevated. The committee continued to expect solid real growth and viewed the labor market as stable. Renewed hostilities therefore landed on an existing branch of the reaction function, moving expected tightening forward without demonstrating that every official had chosen that branch. June CPI on July 14 becomes especially important because non-energy inflation will determine whether oil is amplifying a broader problem.

Energy-market detail kept that risk live through the close. WTI ended at $73.52, up 4.37%, and Brent at $78.02 after rising above $79 intraday. Commercial US crude stocks increased 3.0 million barrels in the week ended July 3, but gasoline inventories fell 1.9 million and distillates dropped 5.0 million. Distillate stocks stood about 12% below their five-year seasonal average, and refinery utilization reached 95.8%. A crude build alongside large product draws implies that refinery output and final-fuel availability, not the headline crude balance alone, govern near-term inflation transmission.

Domestic activity evidence pulled in opposite directions. May wholesale sales rose 3.4% to $817.4 billion after April’s increase was revised to 2.2%, while inventories edged up only 0.1% to $941.8 billion. The inventories-to-sales ratio fell to 1.15 from 1.31 a year earlier. Strong nominal turnover and a leaner stock cushion supported the view that firms were not facing a broad demand collapse, preserving a real-yield floor and the possibility that supply shocks could pass through quickly.

Consumer credit offered the clearest duration-friendly counterpoint. Total credit was unchanged at a seasonally adjusted annual rate in May after a revised 4.9% April increase. Revolving credit contracted at a 4.7% annual rate, nonrevolving credit grew 1.6%, and seasonally adjusted outstanding credit was $5.1545 trillion. The reversal in card borrowing raises a question about consumption resilience under higher rates. It did not dominate a session centered on oil and the Fed, but it kept the growth side of the policy debate from disappearing.

Treasury’s 10-year reopening demonstrated that concession still attracted end demand. The 9-year 10-month note was awarded at 4.580%, about 0.6 basis points below its pre-auction indication. Bid-to-cover was 2.59, indirect bidders received $31.721 billion, direct bidders $4.175 billion and primary dealers $3.027 billion. The security settles July 15. Strong indirect participation constrained the benchmark selloff, while the absence of a broad rally showed that one auction could absorb scheduled supply without clearing the macro risk premium.

Cash demand was also orderly at the four-month point. The $72 billion 119-day bill auction stopped at a 3.790% high rate with 3.41 bid-to-cover and settles July 14. Indirect bidders received $48.986 billion of competitive awards, compared with $3.158 billion for directs and $18.227 billion for dealers. In the closing curve, the 17-week coupon-equivalent yield rose two basis points to 3.89%, while four-, six- and eight-week yields declined. Firm auction participation and modest very-front-end richening separated available cash from the repricing farther out toward the expected policy path.

The day consequently left the Fed and supply channels distinct. Oil and the minutes raised the return required around two to five years; real yields, not breakevens, carried the benchmark adjustment; and successful auctions showed that the market would absorb both bills and duration at prevailing levels. The unresolved risk sits in persistence. A lasting energy disruption or firm core inflation could validate the earlier hike path, whereas weaker household demand and softer non-energy prices could turn Wednesday’s belly cheapening into an overstatement of policy durability.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit weakened modestly as rates rose and geopolitical risk returned. LQD fell 0.19%, HYG declined 0.13%, and JNK lost 0.16%. The larger high-grade price decline fit its greater duration exposure, while the similar, limited losses in the two high-yield funds argued against a broad flight from lower-quality risk. Cash credit-default swap spreads widened after the ceasefire rupture, giving a more direct sign that tight valuations were being asked to absorb renewed event risk.

The current starting point remained unusually rich. US nontraditional bond funds had grown about 6% during 2026 and 18% since the end of 2024, while European global flexible-bond assets were almost 10% higher for the year. That broader discretionary buyer base can support funding when benchmark valuations offer little cushion. It can also intensify issuer and security selection when geopolitical volatility rises, because unconstrained managers are not required to hold every component of a broad index.

Amazon’s prior-session $25 billion bond sale remained the clearest measure of saturation in high-grade technology. The deal attracted about $41 billion of orders, or 1.6 times its size, compared with 4.7 times for an earlier February transaction. Amazon had issued more than $90 billion during the year, contributing to $194 billion of hyperscaler debt. Bank of America analysts estimated that the sale widened outstanding hyperscaler spreads by six to 15 basis points and added eight basis points to Tuesday’s increase in the 10-year Treasury yield.

Those estimates clarify the inherited supply pressure rather than creating a second Wednesday transaction. Buyers funded Amazon, yet repeated AI-capital-expenditure issuance was large enough to affect relative value outside the issuer’s own curve. Wednesday’s well-bid Treasury reopening showed that benchmark duration could draw demand after the concession. The combination points to finite, price-sensitive balance-sheet capacity: sovereign paper and highly rated technology credit both remained financeable, but neither could assume effortless absorption at the prior clearing level.

Distressed credit supplied a much harsher form of differentiation. An ad hoc group holding more than half of Aston Martin’s senior secured notes signed a cooperation agreement as earnings pressure drove the company’s debt lower. Its $1.05 billion of secured notes due in 2029 traded near 72 cents on the dollar, down about four cents on Wednesday. The security’s senior position did not insulate it from operating weakness, and coordinated creditors increased the importance of recovery value and restructuring leverage over broad market beta.

Victoria’s restructuring showed how access can be restored only through a substantial redistribution of claims. Consenting holders of notes due in 2028 were offered 75% of their value in new second-priority bonds due in 2031 plus about 19% of the equity. The transaction was designed to reduce debt and preferred-share liabilities by more than £300 million. Holders that did not consent faced a write-down to zero. This was liability repair through maturity extension, lower claim value and equity participation, not ordinary refinancing at a wider spread.

Primary access therefore remained open at the top of the quality spectrum and conditional elsewhere. Higher Treasury yields weighed on total returns, renewed Iran risk widened protection costs, and exceptional hyperscaler supply continued to shape high-grade relative value. At the distressed end, creditor organization and claim priority dominated. The common mechanism was a smaller valuation cushion: borrowers could still obtain or restructure funding, but price, collateral and balance-sheet concessions mattered more than the broad availability of cash.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Global bonds reinforced the US repricing before New York opened. Ten-year gilt yields rose 10 basis points to 4.95%, their highest in nearly a month, as traders added to expectations of further increases by major central banks. New Zealand delivered the clearest realized policy step, raising its Official Cash Rate 25 basis points to 2.5%, the first increase in three years, and signaling that more withdrawal of stimulus could be needed because inflation remained above target and activity was expected to strengthen. The decision lifted the currency and local yields and added to the global real-rate impulse reaching Treasuries.

Japan remained the deeper long-duration concern. Ten- and 20-year JGB yields reached their highest levels since 1996, while the yen traded around 162.42 per dollar, near a 40-year low. A revised draft of the government’s annual economic plan referred to monetary policy that supports stable price increases, softening an earlier formulation seen as discouraging BOJ tightening. The wording gave the central bank more latitude, yet the market still had to reconcile currency weakness and inflation with political preference for a strong economy.

Domestic credit conditions supported continued normalization. Japanese bank lending rose 6.3% from a year earlier in June, the fastest pace since August 2020, even after the BOJ lifted its policy rate to 1%. Financial conditions therefore remained accommodative, and stronger loan growth weakened the argument that higher borrowing costs had already choked investment or household activity. With the real policy rate still below zero, the growth evidence kept upward pressure on Japan’s rate path and reduced the relative appeal of foreign duration for some domestic investors.

Fiscal policy complicated the same curve. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s plan envisaged more than ¥370 trillion of public and private investment over 14 years, with funding details still unclear. Moody’s retained Japan’s A1 rating and stable outlook, citing sustained growth, inflation and progress that could lower debt to below 195% of GDP by 2030 from about 219% in fiscal 2020. The rating view tempered immediate solvency anxiety, but it did not resolve how recurring spending, defense needs and reduced BOJ purchases would be financed.

The tension lies between potentially productive investment and the amount of duration the market must carry. A plan that attracts private capital and raises trend growth could strengthen the sovereign profile. A shift toward recurring expenditure without a credible consolidation path would raise borrowing costs and test the buyer base. The government’s removal of an explicit reference to fiscal consolidation made that distinction more salient. Higher JGB yields and a weak yen transmit into Treasuries through hedged relative value, global term premium and the policy response to imported inflation.

European policy remained dispersed. Romania held its benchmark at 6.5% as inflation above 10% and higher oil delayed easing, while Poland kept its rate at 3.75% for a fourth meeting. Poland’s bank raised its projected inflation range for 2026 to 2.4%-3.3% from 1.6%-2.9%, even though June inflation had slowed to 2.5%. These cases show how energy risk can interrupt an easing narrative without producing a uniform hiking cycle: domestic inflation starting points and fiscal credibility determine whether restraint is maintained or intensified.

China offered a counterexample in sovereign absorption. Demand for a 10-year government bond auction reached a record gauge, with an average yield of 1.7249% and a high yield of 1.7418%, close to the roughly 1.728% secondary-market level. Banks were seeking allocations amid insufficient private credit demand. That powerful local bid reflected weak loan creation rather than a global appetite for duration, limiting its ability to offset the upward pressure from New Zealand, Europe and Japan on US yields.

The global picture was therefore one of higher policy and fiscal dispersion, not synchronized funding stress. New Zealand tightened, European central banks stayed alert to inflation, and Japan combined easy real financial conditions with a demanding fiscal path. China absorbed sovereign paper because alternative credit demand was weak. US Treasuries inherited the higher global real-rate hurdle, but Wednesday’s domestic 10-year auction showed that cross-border and institutional buyers still responded when yields supplied enough compensation.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market dollar debt faced a mild common-rate headwind without a new dollar squeeze. EMB fell 0.16%, while the US Dollar Index slipped 0.09% to 101.05. Higher Treasury real yields and renewed oil risk weighed on hard-currency valuations, but the softer dollar limited the funding shock. Country outcomes therefore turned more heavily on reserve access, commodity exposure and each borrower’s debt-management plan than on a uniform tightening in dollar liquidity.

Argentina reduced near-term rollover risk by assembling cash outside international bond markets. The government said it had secured funding for a $4.3 billion semiannual principal-and-interest payment on foreign-currency bonds, including about $4 billion already held in Treasury dollar deposits and multilateral-backed proceeds due before Thursday’s maturity. Since March it had raised roughly $4 billion through locally issued dollar bonds. Officials also identified financing intended to avoid an external-market return through the end of 2027.

Avoiding global issuance shields Argentina from a clearing level it regards as expensive, yet it transfers risk into local dollar placements, official financing and the durability of cash reserves. The payment demonstrates capacity at the current maturity; it does not by itself create a repeatable market-access channel. The sovereign’s debt path remains sensitive to whether multilateral support and domestic dollar demand can cover later obligations without depleting liquidity or forcing a return to higher-cost external bonds.

Bangladesh was considering the opposite transition: a debut overseas sovereign bond to diversify beyond domestic banks and concessional loans. An inter-ministerial panel was tasked with assessing structure, timing and feasibility, with conventional dollar debt the main focus and a panda bond also discussed. Size and currency had not been determined. Planned spending was set to rise 19% from a year earlier, so a debut could establish a benchmark for corporate borrowers while also exposing the sovereign’s fiscal and reform record to external pricing discipline.

Johannesburg illustrated the link between arrears, intergovernmental transfers and prospective capital-market access. The city planned to pay creditors to restore 3.6 billion rand of withheld government funding and was discussing a bond sale for infrastructure repairs. Further disbursement depended on plans to reduce unauthorized, irregular and wasteful expenditure by at least 25% by the end of September and arrange creditor payments. Any bond transaction would therefore rest on fiscal controls and restored transfers, not solely on the urgency of the investment need.

Local inflation added another layer of dispersion. Chilean consumer prices were flat in June against expectations for a 0.2% decline, and annual inflation accelerated to 4.3%, above the 3% target. Food rose even as energy and transportation prices fell, showing that softer fuel was not producing broad disinflation. Market-implied rates still assigned a chance to a quarter-point cut within six months, leaving Chilean duration sensitive to whether the upside surprise persists. Romania’s double-digit inflation and rating risk presented a more severe version of the same oil-fiscal interaction.

Debt-management capacity, rather than one aggregate EM direction, defined the session. Argentina prefunded a near maturity while deferring international issuance; Bangladesh explored a first external benchmark; Johannesburg tied funding access to arrears and spending discipline; and Chile confronted sticky domestic prices. Stable dollar trading softened the shared external constraint, while higher US real yields and oil volatility preserved a demanding backdrop for issuers with thin reserves or near-term refinancing needs.

## Cross asset

Cross assets reflected an inflation shock that stopped short of broad liquidation. The S&P 500 fell 0.28% after recovering from an intraday drop of as much as 1.1%, while the Nasdaq 100 gained 0.27% and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index rose 2.23%. Energy producers benefited from higher crude, and dip buying in technology limited the damage. That divergence supported the rates market’s emphasis on inflation and policy persistence over a sudden collapse in growth expectations.

Volatility rose 4.77% to 16.90, a meaningful daily increase from a still-contained level. The combination of a softer broad equity index, stronger semiconductors and modest losses in high-yield funds described selective event-risk repricing. A deeper growth shock would normally have produced a clearer Treasury haven rally and broader equity weakness. Instead, bonds sold off as oil raised the prospective policy response, while risk assets differentiated according to sector exposure.

Oil was the decisive cross-market bridge. WTI’s rise to $73.52 and Brent’s advance to $78.02 reversed part of their substantial one-month declines, while gasoline and distillate draws showed why refined-product prices could respond more strongly than the crude build suggested. The renewed conflict also stranded around 63 million barrels of Iranian oil already on the water after the US revoked a sales waiver. The near-term risk came from disrupted marketability and shipping, even though absolute crude prices remained below earlier wartime peaks.

Gold fell 1.79% to $4,070.90 despite the renewed geopolitical threat. Rising real yields increased the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding asset, and positioning pressure outweighed an immediate haven bid. The dollar also failed to rally, easing slightly to 101.05 even as Fed hike expectations moved forward. Those moves complicate a simple risk-off reading: the market demanded more real yield in Treasuries without simultaneously seeking broad protection through the dollar or gold.

The full constellation reinforced a contained inflation-policy shock. Oil, volatility and lower investment-grade bond prices aligned with higher yields, yet semiconductors rallied, high-yield losses were small and the dollar weakened. The main unresolved question is persistence. If energy disruption lasts long enough to affect consumer prices and non-energy inflation, the relative resilience of equities and credit may be tested. If supply flows normalize, flat breakevens and successful Treasury absorption leave room for the real-rate premium to retrace.

## Forward Market Significance

Initial unemployment claims on July 9 at 8:30 a.m. ET, existing-home sales at 10:00 a.m., and June CPI with real earnings on July 14 at 8:30 a.m. form one policy-path test. Stable claims and resilient rate-sensitive housing, followed by firm core and non-energy inflation, would give the FOMC minutes a realized domestic counterpart and keep two- to five-year yields exposed to an October hike path. Weakening labor flows, softer housing turnover and cooler underlying CPI would elevate May’s contraction in revolving credit, challenge the belly-led cheapening, and recast the oil move as a headline shock that need not sustain restrictive real rates. Real earnings will also show whether higher energy costs are beginning to erode household purchasing power, linking the inflation result back to consumption rather than treating prices and growth as separate signals.

The $22 billion reopening of the 29-year 10-month 5% Treasury bond on July 9 at 1:00 p.m. ET is the next direct test of duration capacity, with settlement on July 15 alongside the 10-year note sold Wednesday. A stop through contemporaneous pre-auction trading, broad direct and indirect participation, limited dealer retention and stable secondary performance would extend the 10-year auction’s message that yield concession can absorb long supply. A weak result or renewed post-sale cheapening would reopen the long-end concern carried from July 7, especially after Amazon’s jumbo financing demonstrated that corporate issuance can compete with sovereign duration for balance-sheet capacity. The 30-year yield, long-end real yields and 2s30s slope are the sensitive channels because they separate near-term Fed repricing from the fiscal, buyer-base and supply compensation embedded beyond the policy horizon.

The BOJ regional economic report on July 9, Bank Negara Malaysia’s decision later that day, and German final CPI plus Canada’s labor report on July 10 define the international persistence test. Broader Japanese wage and price-setting strength, a more hawkish Malaysian message, firm German services inflation or resilient Canadian employment would reinforce New Zealand’s hike and keep global real yields, currencies and Treasury duration under pressure. Softer regional Japanese demand, continued Malaysian patience, benign German composition and weaker Canadian labor conditions would challenge the idea of a synchronized tightening impulse, even if oil remains volatile. Japan carries the greatest cross-market weight: evidence that activity can tolerate further normalization would raise JGB yields and alter hedged demand for Treasuries, whereas a weaker report could resolve part of the global-duration pressure without answering Japan’s longer-run fiscal funding question.

section_summaries.market_read: Real-rate persistence, not generalized stress, remained the defining fixed-income signal.
section_summaries.next_check: Energy persistence must be weighed against the durability of benchmark auction demand.
section_summaries.summary: The close extended restrictive-rate repricing, led by the policy-sensitive belly as renewed energy risk and hawkish Fed communication advanced expected tightening. Strong 10-year auction demand, unchanged benchmark inflation compensation and resilient risk assets contained the move, distinguishing higher required real compensation from a broader funding or inflation-anchor break.

section_summaries.market_read: The belly now bears the clearest risk from a persistent shift toward renewed Fed tightening.
section_summaries.next_check: Underlying CPI will show whether oil is reinforcing broader inflation or only headline pressure.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Five-year underperformance and modest curve flattening marked a policy-path adjustment rather than long-end fiscal steepening. Real yields carried benchmark cheapening while breakevens held, and solid note and bill auctions showed cash and duration demand remained available at higher clearing levels.

section_summaries.market_read: Credit remained open but differentiated, with financing terms doing more work than cash scarcity.
section_summaries.next_check: Further large issuance will test whether investors can absorb duration without wider concessions.
section_summaries.summary: Credit absorbed higher rates and renewed geopolitical risk with only modest broad losses, but tight valuations left little cushion. Heavy hyperscaler issuance exposed price-sensitive balance-sheet capacity, while distressed restructurings showed funding access increasingly depended on collateral, claim priority and meaningful creditor concessions.

section_summaries.market_read: Global policy dispersion raised US duration’s clearing hurdle without signaling synchronized stress.
section_summaries.next_check: Japanese activity and inflation must confirm whether normalization can keep pressuring global yields.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: New Zealand’s hike, inflation vigilance in Europe and Japan’s weak yen and rising yields lifted the global real-rate hurdle for Treasuries. China’s strong sovereign demand reflected weak private credit rather than broad duration appetite, while successful US issuance showed buyers still responded to sufficient yield compensation.

section_summaries.market_read: Stable dollar trading softened the common shock, leaving reserve and rollover capacity in focus.
section_summaries.next_check: Future payments must show whether domestic dollars and official support can preserve liquidity.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: EM dollar debt faced higher US real yields and oil volatility without a broad dollar squeeze, making country funding plans decisive. Argentina prefunded a maturity, Bangladesh explored external issuance, Johannesburg linked access to fiscal repair, and Chile’s inflation surprise complicated prospective easing.

section_summaries.market_read: Risk assets differentiated around energy exposure rather than confirming a broad growth shock.
section_summaries.next_check: The key check is whether energy disruption reaches consumer prices and weakens risk resilience.
section_summaries.summary: Cross assets priced a contained inflation-policy shock: oil and volatility rose, broad equities softened, yet technology and semiconductors recovered while high-yield losses stayed limited. Higher real yields weighed on gold without lifting the dollar, arguing against a simple liquidation or haven-demand regime.

section_summaries.market_read: Persistence now depends on realized inflation, long-bond absorption and global normalization.
section_summaries.next_check: The first confirmation will come from July 9 US data and the long-bond reopening.
section_summaries.summary: Three tests will determine whether the real-rate repricing persists: domestic labor, housing and CPI data; the 30-year Treasury reopening; and international policy signals led by Japan. Their outcomes will separate durable Fed and global tightening pressure from a temporary oil shock and identify whether long-duration demand remains intact.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.31% (+1 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.25% (-0 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 35 bp (-1 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 85 bp (-1 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Treasury belly leads a controlled selloff as oil and Fed repricing lift real-rate pressure
overall_summary.session_narrative: The NY close extended the prior session’s selloff, with the five-year sector underperforming as oil supply risk and the June FOMC minutes reinforced a more persistent restrictive-policy path. Benchmark cheapening came through higher real yields rather than wider inflation compensation. Strong demand at the 10-year reopening and a late equity recovery limited long-end and funding stress, while energy persistence remained the key constraint.
overall_summary.summary: Treasuries cheapened most in the policy-sensitive belly as renewed energy risk and hawkish Fed minutes pulled expected tightening forward. A strong 10-year reopening, flat benchmark inflation compensation and resilient risk assets kept the move contained rather than disorderly.

watch_events.market_implication: Two- to five-year yields would remain exposed; softer data would challenge the belly selloff.
watch_events.market_trigger: Stable labor and housing with firm non-energy inflation would reinforce an October hike path.
watch_events.title: US labor, housing and inflation test
watch_events.watch_line: Track claims and home sales on July 9, then core inflation and real earnings on July 14.

watch_events.market_implication: A weak sale would pressure long yields and curve slope through renewed supply compensation.
watch_events.market_trigger: A through-market stop with broad end demand would extend the 10-year absorption signal.
watch_events.title: Thirty-year Treasury reopening
watch_events.watch_line: Watch auction clearing, investor participation, dealer retention and secondary performance.

watch_events.market_implication: Higher global real yields, led by Japan, would pressure Treasury duration and hedged demand.
watch_events.market_trigger: Firm activity or inflation and hawkish policy signals would confirm broader normalization.
watch_events.title: International policy persistence test
watch_events.watch_line: Track Japanese regional activity, Malaysia’s decision, German inflation and Canadian jobs.
